An employment contract sets out the essential conditions for paid work, such as salary, working hours, and annual leave. There are two types of employment contracts in Germany: fixed-term and open-ended. A typical working week in Germany comprises between 35 and 40 hours. Employers who offer their employees such opportunities can expand their skills, increase their motivation and strengthen their loyalty to the company. Part-time studies: In this case, employees can acquire an academic degree (Bachelor’s or Master’s) in addition to their regular job, which can be supported by their employer. Depending on the prior agreement between employer and employee, appropriately funded employees are obliged to work for the company after graduation. German Federal Statistical Office","content":"# Quality of employment Vacation entitlement. The indicator shows the average number of days of vacation entitlement per em­ployee in a calendar year. The vacation entitlement is not the same as the number of vacation days ac­tu­ally taken. ## Vacation entitlement. According to the Federal Holidays Act, all employees in Germany with a 5-day week are entitled to a minimum of 20 vacation days per year. In most economic branches, full-time workers were entitled to at least 28 vacation days in 2018. The highest vacation entitlement was reported by the branches, energy and water supply fi­nan­cial and in­su­rance activities, public administration as well as education with an average of 30 days for full-time employees. In agriculture and forestry (24 days) and accommodation and food service activities (25 days) the average vacation entitlement was markedly smaller. It is recommended to examine results only by full-time employees as the different part time models of part-time employees affects the average number of vacation days.","score":0.529045,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":0.85,"request_id":"f091ec67-979d-46f9-a5c7-8359995f7174"}

turns.result_full: {"query":"German Federal Vacation Act minimum annual leave days 2024 legal requirement","follow_up_questions":null,"answer":null,"images":[],"results":[{"url":"https://zmi.de/en/lexikon/minimum-vacation/","title":"Minimum leave in Germany - legal entitlement","content":"The minimum leave in Germany is regulated by law in the Federal Leave Act (Bundesurlaubsgesetz – BUrlG) and ensures that employees receive paid time off each year to recuperate. According to § 3 BUrlG, the statutory minimum vacation entitlement is 24 working days per year. For employees with a 5-day week, the minimum leave is reduced accordingly to 20 working days per year. This regulation enables new employees to take vacation in their first year of employment, even if the full entitlement has not yet been earned. * **Severely disabled people**: In addition to the statutory minimum leave, they are entitled to five additional days of leave per year. In addition, employers must have informed their employees transparently and unambiguously of the existing leave entitlement and the possibility of forfeiture in good time before it expires. With clear legal regulations and the support of modern time management systems, companies can ensure fair and transparent vacation planning that meets both operational requirements and the needs of employees.","score":0.99989367,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.jomec.nl/post/vacation-in-germany","title":"Something You Need to Know about the Vacation in Germany - Jomec","content":"According to § 3 Sec. 1 of this Act the minimum vacation are 24 days per year. However, as this is counted on the basis of a six working days","score":0.99958915,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.destatis.de/EN/Themes/Labour/Labour-Market/Quality-Employment/Dimension2/2_2_VacationEntitlement.html","title":"Vacation entitlement - German Federal Statistical Office","content":"# Quality of employment Vacation entitlement. The indicator shows the average number of days of vacation entitlement per em­ployee in a calendar year. The vacation entitlement is not the same as the number of vacation days ac­tu­ally taken. ## Vacation entitlement. According to the Federal Holidays Act, all employees in Germany with a 5-day week are entitled to a minimum of 20 vacation days per year. In most economic branches, full-time workers were entitled to at least 28 vacation days in 2018. The highest vacation entitlement was reported by the branches, energy and water supply fi­nan­cial and in­su­rance activities, public administration as well as education with an average of 30 days for full-time employees. In agriculture and forestry (24 days) and accommodation and food service activities (25 days) the average vacation entitlement was markedly smaller. It is recommended to examine results only by full-time employees as the different part time models of part-time employees affects the average number of vacation days.","score":0.9994429,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.gloroots.com/leave-policy/germany","title":"Leave Policy in Germany: Leave Regulations & Holidays - Gloroots","content":"# Leave Policy in Germany. Explore and manage Germany's holidays and leave types with Gloroots, streamlining annual leave, holiday requests, and employee leave efficiently. In Germany, the Federal Holidays Act (Bundesurlaubsgesetz) mandates a minimum of 20 days of annual leave for employees on a 5-day workweek and 24 days for those on a 6-day workweek. * Eligible employees in Germany who have been employed for at least four weeks before taking sick leave receive full pay (100% of regular income) for up to six weeks under the policy of continued payment of wages during illness (Entgeltfortzahlung bei Krankheit oder Kur). * Pregnant employees in Germany are entitled to 14 weeks of paid maternity leave under the Maternity Protection Act (Mutterschutzgesetz – MuSchG), which extends to 18 weeks in cases of complicated or multiple births. Germany's national labor law does not ensure a statutory right to casual leave. * Emergency care for a close relative: Employees may receive up to 10 days of unpaid leave.","score":0.99890983,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.twobirds.com/en/insights/2024/germany/entstehung-und-abgeltung-von-urlaubsanspruechen-urlaubsanspruch-bei-doppelarbeitsverhaeltnisse","title":"Accrual and settlement of vacation entitlements - Vacation entitlement in the case of dual employment relationships - Bird & Bird","content":"Particularly in the case of short notice periods, it is therefore possible that an employee first has the full annual entitlement at the beginning of the calendar year, then terminates the employment within the first half of the calendar year so that he has already taken more vacation days than he or she is entitled to under a pro-rata calculation. In both cases, the law is based on the principle that the statutory vacation entitlement must be granted (and taken) in the calendar year, even across employers: According to Section 6 (1) German Federal Leave Act, an entitlement to vacation is excluded to the extent that vacation entitlements for the relevant calendar year have already been granted by a previous employer. Accordingly, according to the Federal Labor Court, the leave received in the new employment relationship cannot be offset against the leave entitlements against the original employer by way of an overall calculation based on the period in which both employment relationships existed, but only in relation to the calendar year.","score":0.9981324,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":0.77,"request_id":"9eb88438-427d-40a0-8388-33cc13d875dd"}

final_answer:
Based on the search results, the legal minimum annual vacation in Germany is 20 days for a 5-day workweek, as mandated by the Federal Leave Act (Bundesurlaubsgesetz). Inazuma's policy provides 30 days of Earned Leave per year, which exceeds this legal requirement.

Citations:
- "Inazuma.co Leave Policy provides 30 days of Earned Leave (EL) per year for regular employees" (triple #103564, source #40125)
- "According to § 3 BUrlG, the statutory minimum vacation entitlement in Germany is 24 working days per year. For employees with a 5-day week, the minimum leave is reduced accordingly to 20 working days per year" (web: https://zmi.de/en/lexikon/minimum-vacation/)
